front: In Fourth Amendment law, what is "curtilage"?
back: The area immediately surrounding and associated with the home — the land to which the activity of home life extends.

front: For Fourth Amendment purposes, how is curtilage treated relative to the home?
back: As part of the home itself — so a physical intrusion onto curtilage to gather evidence is a search, presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.

front: What Fourth Amendment protection do "open fields" (land beyond the curtilage) receive?
back: None — open fields receive no Fourth Amendment protection at all.

front: Distinguish curtilage from open fields for Fourth Amendment purposes.
back: Curtilage is part of the home and is protected (intrusion to gather evidence = a search); open fields lie beyond the curtilage and get no Fourth Amendment protection at all.

front: What test decides whether a given spot is curtilage (protected) or open fields (not protected)?
back: The four-factor Dunn test.

front: What did Hester v. United States (1924) establish about open fields?
back: It is the origin of the open-fields doctrine: the Fourth Amendment's protection of "persons, houses, papers, and effects" does not extend to open fields.

front: Which 1924 Supreme Court case is the origin of the open-fields doctrine, holding the Fourth Amendment does not reach open fields?
back: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. 57 (1924).

front: What did Oliver v. United States (1984) hold about open fields?
back: It reaffirmed Hester: there is no legitimate expectation that open fields will be free from warrantless intrusion — only the curtilage carries the home's Fourth Amendment protection.

front: Under Oliver, does fencing land or posting "No Trespassing" signs convert open fields into protected space?
back: No — even fenced land posted with "No Trespassing" signs remains open fields with no Fourth Amendment protection.

front: Oliver: "[O]pen fields do not provide the setting for those {{intimate activities}} that the Amendment is intended to shelter from government interference or surveillance."
back: intimate activities
cite: Oliver v. United States, 466 U.S. at 179

front: What did California v. Ciraolo (1986) hold about warrantless naked-eye aerial observation of a fenced curtilage from navigable airspace?
back: It is not a Fourth Amendment search — there is no reasonable expectation of privacy from observation in public navigable airspace (here, 1,000 feet).

front: Officers in a plane at 1,000 feet observe marijuana with the naked eye in a fenced backyard. Which case holds this is not a search?
back: California v. Ciraolo, 476 U.S. 207 (1986).

front: State the four-factor Dunn test for distinguishing curtilage from open fields (verbatim).
back: Curtilage questions are "resolved with particular reference to four factors: the proximity of the area claimed to be curtilage to the home, whether the area is included within an enclosure surrounding the home, the nature of the uses to which the area is put, and the steps taken by the resident to protect the area from observation by people passing by."

front: What is Dunn factor 1?
back: The proximity of the area claimed to be curtilage to the home.
cite: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. at 301

front: What is Dunn factor 2?
back: Whether the area is included within an enclosure surrounding the home.
cite: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. at 301

front: What is Dunn factor 3?
back: The nature of the uses to which the area is put.
cite: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. at 301

front: What is Dunn factor 4?
back: The steps taken by the resident to protect the area from observation by people passing by.
cite: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. at 301

front: Are the Dunn factors a mechanical checklist?
back: No — they are not a mechanical checklist; each bears on the central question: is the area so intimately tied to the home that it should be treated as part of it?
cite: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. at 301

front: What central question do all four Dunn factors ultimately serve?
back: Whether the area is so intimately tied to the home that it should be treated as part of it.
cite: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. at 301

front: On the facts of Dunn, was the barn (about 60 yards from the house and outside the fence surrounding the house) curtilage?
back: No — applying the four factors, the barn was held not to be curtilage.

front: Which 1987 Supreme Court case sets the four-factor test for distinguishing curtilage from open fields?
back: United States v. Dunn, 480 U.S. 294 (1987).

front: In Florida v. Jardines (2013), was the front porch curtilage?
back: Yes — the front porch is curtilage, part of the home for Fourth Amendment purposes.

front: Why was bringing a drug-detection dog onto the front porch in Jardines a search?
back: It exceeded the implied license to approach and knock, so it was a trespass onto curtilage to gather evidence — a search.

front: Under Jardines, what does a visitor's implied license to approach a home typically permit?
back: To approach by the front path, knock promptly, wait briefly to be received, and then (absent invitation to linger) leave.

front: Jardines: the implied license to approach a home "is generally managed without incident by the Nation's {{Girl Scouts and trick-or-treaters}}."
back: Girl Scouts and trick-or-treaters

front: Under Jardines, does the implied license define only the place an officer may go, or also the scope of conduct?
back: It defines the scope, not merely the place — exceeding the scope (e.g., deploying a drug dog or lingering to snoop) turns a lawful approach into a trespassory search.

front: Which case held that bringing a drug-detection dog onto a front porch exceeds the implied knock-and-talk license and is a trespassory search?
back: Florida v. Jardines, 569 U.S. 1 (2013).

front: On which theory of "search" did the Court decide Jardines, and which theory did it expressly decline to reach?
back: It decided on the trespass/property theory; it expressly noted it need not reach the Katz reasonable-expectation-of-privacy theory.

front: Which 2012 case revived the property/trespass theory of search that Jardines then applied?
back: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400 (2012) — a physical intrusion into a constitutionally protected area to gather evidence is itself a search.

front: How do the trespass/property theory and the Katz privacy theory of search relate?
back: Either can independently establish a search; Katz supplements the property baseline — it did not replace it.

front: Distinguish a lawful vantage to observe curtilage from a lawful entry onto it.
back: Officers may look at curtilage from a place they are lawfully entitled to be (e.g., the air or a public road) without it being a search (Ciraolo), but the right to observe curtilage is not the right to enter it to search (Collins).

front: Under Collins v. Virginia (2018), can the automobile exception justify a warrantless entry onto curtilage to search a vehicle parked there?
back: No — the automobile exception does not permit warrantless entry of a home or its curtilage to search a vehicle parked there.

front: In Collins, was the partially enclosed driveway top abutting the house curtilage?
back: Yes — it was curtilage, carrying the home's Fourth Amendment protection.

front: Which case held that the automobile exception cannot be used to enter curtilage, and that a driveway abutting the house was curtilage?
back: Collins v. Virginia, 584 U.S. 586 (2018).

front: What does Collins illustrate about how far the home's protection against warrant exceptions reaches?
back: The strong protection of the home extends out to its curtilage, which carries that protection against warrant exceptions like the automobile exception.

front: Contrast Ciraolo and Collins on what officers may do with respect to curtilage.
back: Ciraolo: officers may observe curtilage from a lawful vantage (the air) without a search. Collins: officers may not physically enter curtilage to search without a warrant. Observation ≠ entry.

front: Common pitfall: are a driveway, porch, or attached carport fair game just because there is no fence?
back: No — proximity plus the home-life connection (not the absence of a fence) controls; Collins held a driveway abutting the house was curtilage, and Jardines held the front porch was.

front: Common pitfall: does Ciraolo mean "anything visible is fair game"?
back: No — Ciraolo permits observation from a lawful vantage; it does not authorize physically entering the curtilage (Collins).

front: Common pitfall: does a fence or "No Trespassing" sign convert open fields into protected space?
back: No — Oliver says it does not; open fields remain unprotected even when fenced and posted.

front: Common pitfall: is the implied "knock-and-talk" license unlimited once an officer is lawfully on the porch?
back: No — the license is scope-limited; overstaying or bringing investigative tools (like a drug dog) onto the porch converts a lawful approach into a search (Jardines).

front: Why does the curtilage/open-fields line matter for suppression?
back: Same officer, same field of contraband — which side of the line the ground falls on decides whether the intrusion is a search and thus whether evidence is suppressed.

front: Do businesses or commercial premises have "curtilage" the way a home does?
back: No — curtilage is a home concept; commercial property has no analogous domestic "curtilage," and the outdoor ground of a business is treated more like open fields than like the curtilage of a house.

front: Because a business has no curtilage, does that mean its premises have no Fourth Amendment privacy at all?
back: No — no curtilage does not mean no privacy. Commercial premises, especially their non-public interiors, still retain a reasonable expectation of privacy.

front: For a business, distinguish the Fourth Amendment treatment of its open outdoor grounds from its private interior.
back: The open, publicly exposed grounds of a business are open-fields-like (lawful-vantage observation is generally fine), but entry into the private, non-public interior of commercial premises still needs a warrant or a recognized exception.

front: What did Dow Chemical Co. v. United States (1986) hold about the open areas of a 2,000-acre industrial complex?
back: Those open areas between buildings are more comparable to an open field than to the curtilage of a home, so warrantless EPA aerial photography from navigable airspace was not a search.
cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227, 235–39 (1986)

front: Did Dow Chemical hold that businesses have zero Fourth Amendment privacy?
back: No — even though the industrial open areas were open-fields-like, the Court acknowledged that commercial premises still retain some Fourth Amendment privacy.
cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227, 235–39 (1986)

front: Which Supreme Court case held that the open areas of a large industrial complex are more like open fields than curtilage, allowing warrantless aerial photography?
back: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227 (1986).
cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227 (1986)

front: What did See v. City of Seattle (1967) hold about commercial premises and warrantless administrative entry?
back: Commercial premises are protected by the Fourth Amendment against unreasonable warrantless administrative entry; the businessman, like the homeowner, may insist on a warrant.
cite: See v. City of Seattle, 387 U.S. 541, 545–46 (1967)

front: Under See v. City of Seattle, how does a businessman's right to demand a warrant before an administrative entry compare to a homeowner's?
back: The businessman, like the homeowner, may insist on a warrant — commercial premises carry comparable protection against warrantless administrative entry.
cite: See v. City of Seattle, 387 U.S. 541, 545–46 (1967)

front: Which 1967 Supreme Court case (companion to Camara) held that commercial premises are protected against warrantless administrative entry?
back: See v. City of Seattle, 387 U.S. 541 (1967).
cite: See v. City of Seattle, 387 U.S. 541 (1967)

front: What did G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States (1977) hold about warrantless entry into a company's private, non-public offices?
back: It was an unreasonable Fourth Amendment search — business interiors not open to the public carry a reasonable expectation of privacy.
cite: G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States, 429 U.S. 338, 351–59 (1977)

front: In G. M. Leasing Corp., where did the Fourth Amendment line fall — given that seizing cars from public areas was upheld?
back: The line falls at entry into the private business interior: seizing cars from open/public areas was upheld, but warrantless entry into the corporation's private offices was unreasonable.
cite: G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States, 429 U.S. 338, 351–59 (1977)

front: Which case held that warrantless entry into a corporation's private offices to seize assets was an unreasonable search, while seizing cars from public areas was upheld?
back: G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States, 429 U.S. 338 (1977).
cite: G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States, 429 U.S. 338 (1977)

front: What is the controlling federal authority for the point that a business has no curtilage yet its private areas can retain a reasonable expectation of privacy?
back: The SCOTUS line: Dow Chemical (industrial open areas are open-fields-like), See v. City of Seattle, and G. M. Leasing Corp. (non-public business interiors carry a reasonable expectation of privacy).

front: The page cites State v. Karston, State v. Weaver, and State v. Larson on the commercial-privacy point — what weight do they carry?
back: They are state — illustrative, non-binding decisions, cited only to show the federal principle (that private business areas can retain a reasonable expectation of privacy) at work; the controlling authority is the SCOTUS line (Dow Chemical; See; G. M. Leasing).

front: Which state — illustrative, non-binding case is cited as a Louisiana curtilage/search illustration on the commercial-privacy point?
back: State v. Karston (La. Ct. App. 1991) (state — illustrative, non-binding), paired with the controlling SCOTUS commercial-privacy line.
cite: State v. Karston, 588 So. 2d 165 (La. Ct. App. 1991)

front: Which state — illustrative, non-binding case (decided by the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals) is cited as a curtilage/commercial-privacy illustration?
back: State v. Weaver (Tex. Crim. App. 2011) (state — illustrative, non-binding), paired with the controlling SCOTUS commercial-privacy line.
cite: State v. Weaver, 349 S.W.3d 521 (Tex. Crim. App. 2011)

front: Which state — illustrative, non-binding case is cited as an Oregon Court of Appeals curtilage/search illustration on the commercial-privacy point?
back: State v. Larson (Or. App. 1999) (state — illustrative, non-binding), paired with the controlling SCOTUS commercial-privacy line.
cite: State v. Larson, 159 Or. App. 34, 977 P.2d 1175 (1999)

front: Common pitfall: for officers, are the open grounds of a business and its private interior treated the same under the Fourth Amendment?
back: No — the open, publicly exposed grounds are open-fields-like (lawful-vantage observation is generally fine), but entry into the private, non-public interior still needs a warrant or a recognized exception.
